summary: This notebook implements a baseline for the MABe mouse behavior detection challenge using XGBoost. It processes 2D tracking coordinates from parquet files, engineering extensive temporal and interaction features scaled by video FPS. It trains separate binary XGBoost classifiers for each action type using StratifiedGroupKFold, optimizes per-action prediction thresholds via Optuna, and converts frame-wise probabilities into contiguous action segments. Key takeaways include the importance of FPS-aware feature scaling, group stratification by video to prevent data leakage, and robust post-processing for sparse, overlapping temporal labels.
competition_flow: Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking data, engineers FPS-scaled temporal and pairwise features, trains per-action binary XGBoost models with StratifiedGroupKFold, optimizes thresholds via Optuna, converts frame-wise probabilities to action segments, and outputs a cleaned submission CSV.
data_processing: Handles missing FPS with fallback to 30.0
Removes overlapping predictions and invalid frames during post-processing
features_engineering: FPS-scaled rolling windows for speed, curvature, position, and variance
Exponential weighted means and speed state binning
Relative distance, lead angles, chase metrics, and speed correlation for pairs
Pairwise body part distances and relative orientation
Ear distance shifts and nose-tail distance lags
ensembling: No model ensembling is used; predictions are combined frame-wise across independent binary classifiers per action, followed by threshold application and segment merging.
insights: FPS-aware scaling is critical for temporal features across videos with different frame rates.
Stratifying by video_id prevents data leakage during cross-validation.
Training binary classifiers per action handles class imbalance better than multiclass approaches.
Optimizing per-action thresholds via Optuna improves F1 over fixed thresholds.
critical_findings: The custom evaluation metric requires careful handling of sparse labels and overlapping predictions.
Videos with no predictions must be filled with dummy segments to avoid submission errors.
Dropping specific headpiece/tail tracking points reduces noise without losing behavioral signal.
